content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

November 2, 2015

Lauren C. Valk
Deputy City Attorney
P.O. Box 481
Santa Cruz, CA 95601-0481

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-15-211

Dear Ms. Valk:

This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Planning Commissioner Tom
Conroy regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act"). Please
note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not
under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or
Section 1090. This letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political Practices Commission
does not act as a finder of fact when it renders advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)

QUESTION

Does the Act prohibit the Planning Commissioner from taking part in the governmental
decision on whether to approve the demolition of the City's existing library and construction (the
"Project") of a new library because there is a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect of the
decision on one or more of his financial interests?

CONCLUSION

No. The Act does not prohibit the Planning Commissioner from taking part in the decision
on whether to approve the Project because there is no reasonably foreseeable financial effect on his
financial interests.

FACTS

Since 2002, the City of Half Moon Bay, San Mateo County, and the San Mateo County
Joint Powers Authority have been working together with citizens groups to replace the
City's existing library with a new library capable of meeting the service needs of the area. The
governmental decision on whether to permit the demolition of the City's existing library and the

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-15-211
Page No. 2

construction of a new, larger library with expanded services on the same site will soon be before the
City's Planning Commission.

Planning Commissioner Conroy is an employee at Kikuchi + Kankel Design Group
("KKDG"), a landscape and architecture firm. KKDG was part of a team that competed with 18
other firms to provide architecture and design services for the library project, but the City did not
select the team for an interview. In August 2015, the City awarded the contract for architecture and
design services to another firm. KKDG has not had any involvement with the library project since it
was part of the team that failed to win the contract for architecture and design services.

The Planning Commissioner's spouse is the President of, and a Member of the Board of
Directors of, The Friends of the Half Moon Bay Library ("The Friends"), a 501(c)(3) nonprofit
organization, and the Chair of the Coastside Library Capital Campaign Committee (the "CLCCC"),
the fundraising arm of The Friends. The Friends supports the City's library by fundraising and
facilitating programs for adults, teens, and children within the community. The Planning
Commissioner's spouse has earned no compensation from, and has received no reportable gifts as a
result of, her volunteer work for The Friends or the CLCCC.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using
his or her position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest.
Section 87103 provides that a public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision,
within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect on one or more of the official's interests. Section 87103 describes financial interests
from which a conflict of interest may arise, and those pertinent to the facts provided include:

• Source of Income – A public official has a financial interest in any source of
income, including promised income, which aggregates to $500 or more within
12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c).)

• Business Entity – A public official has a financial interest in any business
entity in which he or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or
holds any position of management. (Section 87103(d).)

• Personal Finances – A public official has a financial interest in his or her
personal finances, including those of his or her immediate family. This is
known as the "personal financial effects" rule. (Section 87103.)

Planning Commissioner Conroy is an employee at Kikuchi + Kankel Design Group
("KKDG"), Thus, KKDG is an economic interest as a source of income and because the
commissioner is employed by KKDG.

The Planning Commissioner's spouse has earned no compensation from and has
received no reportable gifts as a result of her volunteer work for The Friends or the
CLCCC. Therefore, neither of these organizations is a "source of income" under Section

File No. A-15-211
Page No. 3

87103(c). Section 82005 defines "business entity" as "any organization or enterprise
operated for a profit, including but not limited to a proprietorship, partnership, firm,
business trust, joint venture, syndicate, corporation or association." The Friends is a
nonprofit organization, and the CLCCC is an arm of The Friends. Because these entities
are nonprofit organizations, neither qualifies as a "business entity" under Section
87103(d). Thus, we find that the Planning Commissioner's spouse's volunteer work for
The Friends and the CLCCC is not an interest as contemplated by Section 87103 and
does not give rise to a conflict of interest under the Act.

Next, we must determine whether there is a reasonably foreseeable financial effect of the
decision on one or more of the Planning Commissioner's interests.

Foreseeability:

Under the Act, a conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable
financial effect of a governmental decision on a public official's interests is material. The standard
for foreseeability differs depending on whether an interest is explicitly involved in the decision.
(Regulation 18701.) The Planning Commissioner' interests in KKDG and his interest in his own
personal finances are not explicitly involved in the decision on whether to approve the library
project. Therefore, the applicable foreseeability standard is: "A financial effect need not be likely to
be considered reasonably foreseeable. In general, if the financial effect can be recognized as a
realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical, it is reasonably foreseeable." (Ibid.)

Based on the facts provided, there is no indication that the decision will have a financial
effect on KKDG or the Planning Commissioner's personal finances. KKDG has not had any
involvement with the library project since it was part of a team that failed to win the contract for
architecture and design services. The Planning Commissioner's personal finances do not stand to
increase or decrease as a result of the decision. Thus, we find that there is no apparent reasonably
foreseeable financial effect of the decision on the Planning Commissioner's interests.²

Because there is no reasonably foreseeable financial effect of the decision on the Planning
Commissioner's financial interests, we conclude that the Act does not prohibit him from taking part
in the governmental decision on whether to approve the library project.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 332-5660.

Sincerely,
Hyla P. Wagner
General Counsel
[illegible]
By: Matthew F. Christy
Counsel, Legal Division

² The Commission may decline to provide advice on past conduct (Section 83114(b); Regulation 18329(a)).
Thus, nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any conduct that may have already taken place, and any
conclusions contained in this letter apply only to prospective actions.

sections.question:
Does the Act prohibit the Planning Commissioner from taking part in the governmental 
decision on whether to approve the demolition of the City's existing library and construction (the 
"Project") of a new library because there is a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect of the 
decision on one or more of his financial interests?
sections.conclusion:
No. The Act does not prohibit the Planning Commissioner from taking part in the decision 
on whether to approve the Project because there is no reasonably foreseeable financial effect on his 
financial interests.

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using 
his or her position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest. 
Section 87103 provides that a public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision,

entity in which he or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or 
holds any position of management. (Section 87103(d}.) 
• Personal Finances - A public official has a financial interest in his or her 
personal finances, including those of his or her immediate family. This is 
known as the "personal financial effects" rule. (Section 87103.) 
Planning Commissioner Conroy is an employee at Kikuchi + Kankel Design Group 
("KKDG"), Thus, KKDG is an economic interest as a source of income and because the 
commissioner is employed by KKDG.

received no reportable gifts as a result of her volunteer work for The Friends or the

Under the Act, a conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable

Because there is no reasonably foreseeable financial effect of the decision on the Planning

QUESTION: Does the Act prohibit the Planning Commissioner from taking part in the governmental 
decision on whether to approve the demolition of the City's existing library and construction (the 
"Project") of a new library because there is a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect of the 
decision on one or more of his financial interests?

CONCLUSION: No. The Act does not prohibit the Planning Commissioner from taking part in the decision 
on whether to approve the Project because there is no reasonably foreseeable financial effect on his 
financial interests.
